title: Disciplinary Process Triggers — HR Case Codes Correlated with Security Incidents

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.6.4 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.6.4 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.19: Disciplinary Process Triggers — HR Case Codes Correlated with Security Incidents.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.19' running on snow:hr_case (restricted), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=hr_restricted sourcetype=snow:hr_case earliest=-180d category=conduct
| eval day=round(_time/86400,0)
| join type=inner day [| search index=itsm sourcetype=snow:incident category=security earliest=-180d | eval day=round(_time/86400,0) | stats dc(number) as sec_cases by day]
| stats count by day
description: Correlates anonymized HR case creation timestamps with security investigations to evidence disciplinary process alignment without exposing PII in dashboards.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes hr_restricted, itsm and sourcetypes snow:hr_case, snow:incident. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Strict RBAC on index; (2) legal privacy review; (3) aggregate only; (4) document data minimization. Indexes required: `index=hr_restricted`, `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:hr_case`, `sourcetype=snow:incident`.
visualization: Time chart, Single value. Key fields: `day`, `day]`, `sec_cases`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Bulk onboarding campaigns** — Graduate intake or seasonal hiring creating batch processing that may exceed normal SLA thresholds temporarily.

2. **Cross-border employment checks** — International background screening requiring coordination with foreign authorities having inherently longer processing times.

3. **Contractor agency screening reliance** — Contractors screened by their employment agency with attestation rather than direct screening by the hiring organisation.

4. **Re-hire fast-track** — Former employees returning within 12 months whose previous screening remains valid per policy.

5. **Training completion extensions** — Approved extensions for employees with documented accessibility needs or operational constraints with manager sign-off.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `hr_restricted`, `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.6.4`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.6.4 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.19: Disciplinary Process Triggers — HR Case Codes Correlated with Security Incidents." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `hr_restricted`, `itsm` under `snow:hr_case`, `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=hr_restricted earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Strict RBAC on index. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** legal privacy review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** aggregate only. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** document data minimization. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Disciplinary Process Triggers — HR Case Codes Correlated with Security Incidents," the aggregation summarises incident flow — volume per category, time-to-contain distribution, and resolution cadence are the metrics regulators ask about during post-incident review.

This search correlates `index=hr_restricted` (sourcetype=`snow:hr_case`) on the outer side with `index=itsm` (sourcetype=`snow:incident`) on the inner side, joined on `day` across the last 180 days. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `day` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.19: Disciplinary Process Triggers — HR Case Codes Correlated wit]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -180d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.19,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=hr_restricted sourcetype=snow:hr_case earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `day`, `day]`, `sec_cases`. Run:

```spl
index=hr_restricted sourcetype=snow:hr_case earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table day, day], sec_cases
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.19: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (the key fields):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'ISO 27001 current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Disciplinary Process Triggers — HR Case Codes Correlated with Security Incidents. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Grant a test user elevated access to a controlled system without a corresponding approval record in the access management system; verify the monitoring search detects the unapproved privilege within 1 hour and generates an alert with user, system, and missing approval details.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Grant a test user access following the full approval workflow with manager sign-off, risk assessment, and time-limited duration recorded; verify no access violation alert fires and the access appears as properly authorised in the dashboard.

evidence: Access governance report showing recertification completion rates, privilege changes, approval chain documentation, and anomalous access investigation outcomes.
exclusions: Approved maintenance window access; automated service account operations per register; break-glass access during declared incidents; access within recertification cycle timing; project-based temporary elevation.
